narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 40—above the typical 36.7 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but other parts of your academic record raised concerns for the admissions model. This model looks at past students' profiles to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated your probability of failing at 60%, which tipped the scales toward rejection to minimize the school's risk of low bar passage rates. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 3 school decile ranking, in that order of influence. Your strong LSAT was the most powerful positive force, pushing the model toward seeing you as likely to pass by showing sharp analytical skills that often correlate with bar success. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.50—below the average 3.21—worked against you, signaling to the model a weaker foundation in sustained academic performance compared to typical admitted students. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 3, lower than the average 5.53, suggested inconsistent grades later in your undergrad career relative to peers, further nudging the prediction toward failure risk. Factors like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status aligned with the norm—full-time is the path for over 92% of students—so they didn't sway things much either way. Overall, while your LSAT gave you an edge over many applicants, the gaps in GPA and later decile performance made the model view your bar passage odds as riskier than those of typical successful students, who often have higher GPAs and deciles balancing out their scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer, but a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or a post-bac program to boost your GPA and decile standing, as even small improvements there could flip future predictions. You might also gather strong recommendation letters highlighting your LSAT strengths and resilience, or target schools with more holistic reviews. With targeted steps like these, you can reapply stronger and show the model—and admissions teams—what you're truly capable of.
